creator: matthew@FreeBSD.org
text:
On 19/01/2013 17:54, Gerald Pfeifer wrote:
> Good news: make INSTALL_AS_USER=1 install now works and with an empty
> $PKG_DBDIR a new local.sqlite is created.
> 
> 
> Bad news: pkg add some.txz fails with
>   pkg: /home/gerald/porttest.PKG_DBDIR wrong user or group ownership
>   Failure!
> 
> User and group for the directory look okay, though.
> 
>   ref10-i386:~> ls -lad /home/gerald/porttest.PKG_DBDIR
>   drwxr-xr-x  2 gerald  devel  4096 Jan 19 17:03 /home/gerald/porttest.PKG_DBDIR
>   ref10-i386:~> id -a
>   uid=937(gerald) gid=493(devel) groups=493(devel),937(gerald)

Thank you very much indeed for testing.

Bother.  I can't repeat this in my tests. This is compiling with:

worm:~/src/pkgng:% clang -v
FreeBSD clang version 3.1 (branches/release_31 156863) 20120523
Target: x86_64-unknown-freebsd9.0
Thread model: posix

with

DEBUG_FLAGS='-g -O0'

on

stable/9 r244553

With INSTALL_AS_USER=yes and PKG_DBDIR=${HOME}/pkgng I get local.sqlite
and repo.sqlite created with my own user and group, and pkg info runs fine:

worm:~/src/pkgng:% id -a
uid=1001(matthew) gid=1001(matthew)
groups=1001(matthew),0(wheel),5(operator)

worm:~/src/pkgng:% ls -la ~/pkgng
total 1342
drwxr-xr-x   2 matthew  matthew        4 Jan 19 19:59 ./
drwxr-xr-x  14 matthew  matthew       31 Jan 19 19:12 ../
-rw-r--r--   1 matthew  matthew    57344 Jan 19 19:59 local.sqlite
-rw-r--r--   1 matthew  matthew  1215488 Jan 19 19:57 repo.sqlite

worm:~/src/pkgng:% env PKG_DBDIR=/home/matthew/pkgng INSTALL_AS_USER=1
pkg info
worm:~/src/pkgng:% echo $?
0

pkg add does fail but for a different reason than ownership and perms on
the pkg DB:

worm:~/src/pkgng:% env PKG_DBDIR=/home/matthew/pkgng INSTALL_AS_USER=1
pkg add /tmp/pkg-1.0.4_1.txz
Installing pkg-1.0.4_1...pkg: archive_read_extract(): Can't unlink
already-existing object
pkg: archive_read_next_header(): Can't unlink already-existing object
pkg: unlink(/usr/local/etc/periodic/daily/411.pkg-backup): Permission denied
pkg: unlink(/usr/local/etc/periodic/security/410.pkg-audit): Permission
denied
pkg: unlink(/usr/local/etc/pkg.conf.sample): Permission denied
pkg: unlink(/usr/local/include/pkg.h): Permission denied
[...]
Failed to install the following 1 package(s): /tmp/pkg-1.0.4_1.txz

That is expected for this particular .txz: my UID doesn't have write
permissions on /usr/local.  Compiling a .txz with a custom PREFIX
setting would avoid that problem.

> Interestingly, this even happens with pkg info:
> 
>   ref10-i386:~> env PKG_DBDIR=/home/gerald/porttest.PKG_DBDIR ~/bin/pkg info
>   pkg: /home/gerald/porttest.PKG_DBDIR wrong user or group ownership
> 
> So, I added some instrumentation to libpkg/pkgdb.c directly after this
> error message, and oops!
> 
>   pkg: You are 0/0, the object is 937/493
> 
> Looking at the code above, I then found:
> 
>         if (install_as_user) {  
>                 euid = geteuid();
>                 egid = getegid();
>         } else {
>                 euid = 0;
>                 egid = 0;
>         }
> 
> 
> This does not look right, and it's a regression when in the past neither 
> pkg_add and definitely not pkg_info did these checks.  In fact, it is
> also a regression within pkg itself, where pkg info also used to work
> as a regular user (verified on the ref*.FreeBSD.org cluster).

I don't think your change is correct.

Perhaps the variables could be better named: euid, egid here are the
expected user and group ownership of the various files and directories.
I've changed them to fileowner and filegroup.

In normal use both /var/db/pkg and all the .sqlite files pkg uses within
it should be owned by root:wheel and only writeable by root.  In this
case, allowing non-root to modify the pkg DB would be a security debacle
just waiting to happen.

The change as you have it would mean that an ordinary user could not run
pkg info on a standard system.

The install_as_user flag specifically changes the requirement on
ownership so that a (non-root) user can have a personally owned copy of
a pkgng database.  Implicit in that is the assumption that they're going
to set PKG_DBDIR to put their private DB in other than the default
location, and probably customize PREFIX similarly.

I found and fixed a different bug to do with creating repo.sqlite the
first time ever 'pkg update' was run for a new $PKG_DBDIR, and I have
shamelessly stolen your additions to the error message in
file_mode_insecure().

See:
https://github.com/pkgng/pkgng/commit/55ba492c0a2570065743583031c3e1cae2319b2d

	Cheers,

	Matthew

-- 
Dr Matthew J Seaman MA, D.Phil.
PGP: http://www.infracaninophile.co.uk/pgpkey
